mappings.rationaleAI: Projectile-propelling weapons using chemical propellant correspond to gunpowder weapons (A.2), specifically small arms including musket, rifle, pistol, and machine gun (A.2.d).

Protective covering against weapons corresponds to personal protection (B.1), armor against hand-powered weapons in leather, bronze, and iron (B.1.a), and armor against gunpowder weapons in steel and Kevlar (B.1.b).

Military constructions for territorial defense correspond to fortification (B.2), including permanent fortification before gunpowder (B.2.b), early gunpowder era with bastioned trace (B.2.c), and modern concrete bunkers (B.2.d).
Implements designed to damage or kill correspond to offensive weaponry (A), from early hand-powered weapons (A.1) through gunpowder weapons (A.2).
Vehicles achieving flight correspond to aircraft as weapon platforms (C.4), including fixed-wing airplanes (C.4.a) and helicopters (C.4.b).
Self-propelled vehicles with military origins in 13th-century China correspond to self-propelled missiles (A.4) with rocket propulsion (A.4.b).
The earliest chemical explosive directly corresponds to gunpowder weapons (A.2) and black powder as propellant (A.2.e.i).
Bladed tools overlapping with weapon categories correspond to early shock weapons (A.1.b).
Radio wave detection originally developed as WWII military technology corresponds to electromagnetic sensors in communication, navigation, and weapon guidance (F.1).
Organization, technology, and evolution of weapons from edged weapons through nuclear arms corresponds to offensive weaponry (A), defensive weaponry (B), and weapon platforms (C).
Ships performing warfare roles correspond to surface ships and craft as weapon platforms (C.2), spanning oar-powered through nuclear-powered vessels.
Reactive substances releasing stored energy correspond to explosives including grenades, mines, bombs, and missile warheads (A.3).
Applied engineering practice connects to military engineering including tactical support, strategic support, and ancillary support (D).
The science of metals underpins construction materials for weapons from wood and stone through bronze, iron, and steel (A.1.a, A.2.a).

State-organized armed forces correspond to the full scope of military technology: offensive weaponry (A), defensive weaponry (B), weapon platforms (C), engineering (D), logistics (E), and electronics (F).
Nuclear fission and fusion energy connects to nuclear weapons (A.5) and arms-control debates over weapons-grade material.
Navigation using radar, radio, and GNSS connects to electromagnetic sensors in communication and navigation (F.1).
Directed-energy weapons and laser-guided targeting correspond to the use of laser technology in weapon guidance (F.1).
Graphic depictions of spatial relationships used for military strategy correspond to ancillary support including maps (D.3).
